coredata.dc:title: The use of crude carbon dots as green, low-cost and multifunctional additives to improve the curing, mechanical, antioxidative and fluorescence properties of epoxy natural rubber/silica composites
coredata.prism:publicationName: Composites Part A: Applied Science and Manufacturing
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Chaoying
Li, Liwei
Ji, Haijun
Yang, Hui
Jin, Guangzhi
Jiang, Can
Guo, Pingyun
Zhang, Liqun
Yu, Peng
Wang, Runguo
coredata.dc:description: Rubbers are acknowledged as strategically important and irreplaceable materials in daily life, and rubber additives are indispensable components for the practical application of the rubber vulcanizate. Developing novel rubber additives is highly desirable, and pursuing multifunctional rubber additives has been one of the major tasks in the field of rubber. Herein, the crude carbon dots (CCDs) without any purification were used as rubber additives for epoxy natural rubber/silica system by conventional milling process. Our results revealed that the CCDs have outstanding advantages, they could not only endow the rubber with charming fluorescence and excellent anti-aging capability, but also improve the curing rate and mechanical performance of rubber composite. Moreover, with the incorporation of CCDs, the rolling resistance of the rubber composite could be remarkably reduced, which is promising for the application of green tyres. Overall, this work convincingly provides novel inspiration to develop novel low-cost yet multifunctional additives for rubber.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A. Multifunctional composites
A. Nanocomposites
B. Cure behaviour
B. Mechanical properties

Influence of CCDs on the ENR/silica composites
Anti-aging properties of the rubber composites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFC2104702), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51988102, 52273003, 52103065) and the scientific research fund of Wuhan Institute of Technology (grant number K2021044) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation route for the crude carbon dots gel. (b) Preparation route for the rubber composite.

(a) FTIR spectrum for CCDs. (b, c, d) XPS spectra for CCDs, (b) survey XPS spectrum, (c) N 1 s XPS spectrum, (d) C 1 s XPS spectrum. (e) UV–vis spectra for CCDs. (f) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CCDs solution (0.5 wt%) with different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400 nm. (g, h) TEM image for CCDs. The yellow circle indicates the CCDs. (i) The CCDs gel (50 wt%) exhibits Tyndall effect.
The interaction between CCDs, silica and ENR.

Digital photos of the dumbbell-shaped rubber specimens under daylight (A) and 365 nm-UV exposure (B), rubber films under daylight (C) and UV exposure (D), UV − vis absorption curves (E) and fluorescence emission spectra (F) of the rubber films (Excited wavelength of 360 nm): (a) ENR, (b) ENR/4020, (c) ENR/CCDs-2.5, (d) ENR/CCDs-5, (e) ENR/CCDs-8.
SEM images of the cryo-fractured surfaces for the rubber composites (The yellow circle indicates the filler agglomeration).

Influence of CCDs towards rubber composites: (a) curing curves, (b) stress–strain curves composites, (c) tensile strength and elongation at break, (d) plots of δ* versus λ-1 for the rubber composites, (e) G′-strain curves of the gum rubber composites, (f) Tanδ-strain curves for the rubber vulcanizates.

Anti-aging properties of the rubber composites: (a) UV–vis absorbance for the SA-Fe2+–H2O2 aqueous solution upon gradual addition of CCDs from 0 to 1.2 mg•mL−1, (b) Active oxygen species scavenging activity of CCDs, (c) The stress–strain curves for the rubber composites aging for 3 days, (d) Tensile strength and elongation at break retention, (e) The aging coefficient, (f) The change of the Shore A hardness, (g) The change of the crosslinking density, and (h) The DSC curves for the rubber composites (under O2 atmosphere).
Schematic illustration of the possible antioxidative mechanism of CCDs during rubber degradation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Other rubber ingredients: ZnO 5 phr, Stearic acid 2 phr, Accelerator CZ 1 phr, Accelerator DM 1 phr, Sulfur 1.5 phr. It should be noted that the content of CCDs in the formula refers to the dry weight of CCDs.

head.ce:title: The use of crude carbon dots as green, low-cost and multifunctional additives to improve the curing, mechanical, antioxidative and fluorescence properties of epoxy natural rubber/silica composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430205, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rubbers are acknowledged as strategically important and irreplaceable materials in daily life, and rubber additives are indispensable components for the practical application of the rubber vulcanizate. Developing novel rubber additives is highly desirable, and pursuing multifunctional rubber additives has been one of the major tasks in the field of rubber. Herein, the crude carbon dots (CCDs) without any purification were used as rubber additives for epoxy natural rubber/silica system by conventional milling process. Our results revealed that the CCDs have outstanding advantages, they could not only endow the rubber with charming fluorescence and excellent anti-aging capability, but also improve the curing rate and mechanical performance of rubber composite. Moreover, with the incorporation of CCDs, the rolling resistance of the rubber composite could be remarkably reduced, which is promising for the application of green tyres. Overall, this work convincingly provides novel inspiration to develop novel low-cost yet multifunctional additives for rubber.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rubber is a unique polymer of strategic importance used in daily life, and in many of rubber's most significant applications, including tires and seals, cannot be replaced by other alternative materials. Rubbers are seldom used in their original form. A package of rubber additives, such as sulphur, activators, and antioxidants, etc., are generally necessary to compound with rubbers. As these ingredients have specific roles to play, making their inclusion indispensable [1]. For instance, the antioxidants are widely used to retard the thermal-oxidative-aging process of the rubber matrix and thus prolong the rubber service life [2]. However, many rubber additives have some drawbacks, and some of them are derived from petrochemicals or they are toxic. In particular, many commonly used rubber antioxidants with toxic nature are damaging to human health and the environment. For example, the antioxidant MB (2-mercaptobenzimidazole), which is widely used to retard thermal-oxygen aging for light-colored rubber products, is harmful to aquatic organisms. In addition, many animal experiments show that MB is also significantly toxic to the mammalian thyroid [3]. In this context, developing novel multifunctional, non/low-toxic and environmentally-friendly rubber additives is highly desired.

Carbon dots (CDs), the rising star in the carbon material family, have aroused significant attention because of their unique advantages of charming photoluminescence, nano size, negligible cytotoxicity, excellent bio-compatibility, abundance of raw materials, ease of functionalization, etc [4,5]. CDs have been widely explored in the fields of bioimaging, catalysis, lighting, optoelectronics, sensing, nanomedicine, and energy storage/conversion [6,7]. Recently, many studies demonstrated that the CDs could be used as a tunable and effective antioxidant in the field of biomedical and polymer composites due to their radical scavenging capability [2,8–11]. In general, the formation of side-products during CDs synthesis is inevitable, which calls for the necessity of rigorous purification protocols [4]. The purification methods include silica gel column chromatography, dialysis, and centrifugation. Nevertheless, CDs' nano size (≤10 nm) makes it difficult to separate CDs from the mixtures of CDs and the side-products [11]. And the purification procedures for CDs are expensive, time-consuming, and difficult to be scale-up. Till date, very limited studies reported on the use the CDs along with their byproduct mixtures (denoted as crude carbon dots, CCDs) in rubber composites, [12,13] and to the authors' knowledge, there are no reports using the CCDs as multifunctional additives in the diene rubber-silica system.

In this manuscript, we attempt to use CCDs as novel multifunctional additives (antioxidant, fluorescent agent, accelerator) in rubber-silica system. The CCDs was pre-mixed with water to obtain a CCDs gel, which was then incorporated into the ENR matrix by conventional melt-mixing, the water could weak the interaction among the CCDs particles. Meanwhile, the epoxy group of ENR and the hydroxyl group of silica are ready to interact with the polar group of CCDs, favoring a homogenous CCDs dispersion within ENR matrix. In the high temperature internal mixing process (a conventional rubber processing technique), the water in the rubber system could be evaporated. Therefore, the preparation of ENR/silica/CCDs composites is totally compatible with industrial rubber processing practice. Our results demonstrated that the CCDs could act as fluorescent agent, activator, antioxidant and for ENR composite. The CCDs have large amount of surface functional groups, they could interact with the rubber matrix and silica, therefore the CCDs could act as intelligent interfacial bridge between ENR and silica, which significantly improves the overall filler dispersion and strengthens interfacial adhesion in the rubber composites. Therefore, the CCDs could promote rubber's mechanical performances and reduce the rolling resistance, which is beneficial for a sustainable world. We envisage that this study might provide novel inspiration of utilization of low-cost CCDs in the elastomer and beyond.

Epoxidized natural rubber (grade ENR 25) was provided by Agricultural Products Processing Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences (Guangdong, China). Citric acid monohydrate and diethylenetriamine were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd., China. Silica (Trademark: VN-3) was purchased from Evonik Degussa Co., Ltd. Other chemicals were purchased from local sources.

The preparation route for the crude carbon dots gel is shown in Fig. 1 a, CCDs were prepared by a microwave assisted synthesized method, 10 g citric acid monohydrate and 7.36 g diethylenetriamine were dissolved in 100 mL aqueous solution, the mixture solution was subjected to the microwave irradiation at 700 W for 9 min, and the CCDs was obtained, and a suitable amount of water was added into the CCDs to obtain the CCDs gel with 50 wt% solid content. The preparation route for the rubber composite is illustrated in Fig. 1b. In detail, ENR composites were prepared on a two-roll mill at room temperature. The silica was used as reinforcing filler and was fixed as 50 phr (parts per hundred grams of rubber), the loading of CCDs was variable. A given amount of CCDs gel was dropped on the silica, and the mixture along with ENR, ZnO, and SA were subjected to an internal milling process (50 rpm at 150 °C for 7 min) by an internal mixer. During which the silica was silanized with Si69. Also, the water in the composites could be effectively removed in the internal mixing process. Afterwards, the rubber additives were added into rubber compound by a two-roll mill following the conventional rubber processing procedure. ENR/CCDs-x refers to the composite modified by x phr (parts per hundred parts of gum) CCDs. The detailed compounding formations were listed in Table 1 . The rubber compounds were vulcanizated at 150 °C via an electrically heated press procedure for the optimum curing time (T90 + 3 min) determined by a vulcameter.

Fourier transform infrared testing (FTIR) was performed at room temperature on a Bruker Vertex spectrometer. The curing parameters of the rubber compounds were investigated at 150 °C by a Gotech RH-2000 N vulkameter (Gotech Testing Machines Inc). SEM investigations were conducted on a scanning electron microscope (model: Hitachi S-4800, Hitachi Co., Japan), the samples were plated with a thin gold layer before observations. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained from a Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN with the accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The active oxygen species scavenging capacity of CCDs was evaluated by the Fenton reaction following the related reference [10]. The mechanical properties of the rubber composites were studied on a CMT4104 electronic tensile testing instrument (Shenzhen SANS Test Machine Co., Ltd., China) according to ISO 37–2005, the cross-head speed used was 500 mm/min. Crosslinking density of the rubber composite was determined by the equilibrium swelling method (the toluene was used as solvent) using a protocol reported elsewhere [14,15]. RPA analysis was studied out on a RPA2000 machine (Alpha technologies Co, USA). Strain sweeps of the uncured rubber composites: the temperature, frequency and the range of the strain sweeps were set as 60 °C, 10 Hz and 0–200 %, respectively. After that, the temperature was raised to 150 °C to cure the compound for T90. Then, the temperature was reduced to 60 °C. Strain sweeps of the cured rubber composites: the temperature, frequency and the range of the strain sweeps were set as 60 °C, 10 Hz and 0–42 %, respectively. The rolling resistance of the rubber composite can be assessed by the Tan δ value at 7 % strain. Differential scanning calorimetry analysis (DSC) was studied on a DSC Q100 (TA Instruments, USA). The sample was heated from 100 °C to 280 °C with a heating speed of 3 °C/min under O2 atmosphere (50 mL/min), in the rubber oxidation process, an exothermic peak could be observed. Rubber ageing testing was carried out in an age-circulating oven at 100 °C for 3 days according to GB/T3512-2001 standard. Tensile strength retention, elongation at break retention, and aging coefficient were calculated using the following equations:
(1)
Tensilestrengthretention =
T 1
/
T 2
× 100 %
(2)
Elongationatbreakretention =
E 1
/
E 2
× 100 %
(3)
Agingcoefficient =
(
T 1
×
E 1
)
/
(
T 2
×
E 2
)
× 100 %
where T1 and T2 are the tensile strength of the aged and un-aged samples, respectively. E1 and E2 are the elongation at break of the aged and un-aged samples, respectively.

The CCDs were characterized by various techniques. In the FTIR spectrum of CCDs (Fig. 2 a), the broad absorption peak near 3400 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups, [16] and the absorption bands observed between 1640 ∼ 1770 cm−1 and 1350 ∼ 1460 cm−1 are originated from CO group and CH2 group, respectively [17]. XPS testing was carried out to investigate the CCDs (Fig. 2b, 2c, 2d), N atom exhibits three different chemical environments with binding energies at 399.6 eV (amine groups), 400.3 eV (amide-carbonyl groups), and 401.5 eV (graphitic N), respectively [18]. The FTIR and XPS results demonstrated the CCDs are functionalized with the hydroxy and ammine groups, [18] these functional groups could scavenge free radicals in rubber ageing process and thus improve the rubber anti-ageing performance. [2] Fig. 2e shows the UV–vis spectra for CCDs, the CCDs exhibit two absorption peaks at 240 and 340 nm, which are corresponding to the π → π* and n → π* transitions of certain fluorophores in CCDs, respectively [12]. Fig. 2f revealed that the CCDs emitted bright blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light. The emission wavelength for CCDs is around 450 nm upon UV excitation (365 nm). The TEM characterization (Fig. 2g and h) shown the morphology of the CCDs particles, some CCDs particles have the diameters of about 5 nm, while other CCDs particles have the diameters more than 15 nm, which was due to fact that the CCDs are a mixture of fluorophores, polymer dots, quasi CDs, CDs and large carbon particles [19]. As shown in Fig. 2i, CCDs fluid exhibits an obvious Tyndall effect, indicating that the CCDs could be stably dispersed in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between rubber and filler, and the CCDs dispersion in rubber matrix
ce:section.ce:para: Obviously, uniform CCDs dispersion within rubber matrix is the prerequisite to maximize CCDs' potential towards rubber composite. For instance, the CCDs could act as antioxidants for rubber, [13] and the CCDs' antioxidative efficiency increase with the improvement of CCDs dispersion state, because of higher specific surface area available for quenching the free radicals in rubber aging processes. On the contrary, the aggregated CCDs in rubber matrix would lead to a low CCDs utilization, moreover, the CCDs agglomeration would deteriorate the mechanical performances of the rubber composite. In order to homogeneously disperse the CCDs within ENR matrix, the CCDs were added in form of liquid gel instead of dry solid, because the liquid feeding of CCDs gel allows preserving the individualization state of the molecules/particles in CCDs resulting from their colloidal dispersion in aqueous solution.

Meanwhile, it is expected that there are strong interactions among CCDs, silica and ENR matrix. There are large amounts of Si-OH groups on silica surface, which could interact with the polar groups (hydroxy group and amine group, etc.) on the CCDs surface through H-bonding [12]. Furthermore, the ENR could interact with CCDs through H-bonding and ring opening reaction of the epoxy group. In a word, the ENR and silica could effectively interact with CCDs (Fig. 3 ), thus preventing the fine CCDs particles from aggregation together again during milling process. Actually, similar studies that incorporated the wet filler instead of dry one into rubber matrix have been reported in other rubber/filler system [20–23]. For instance, Jiang et al. prepared ENR/lignin composite, [20] wet lignin instead of dry one was milled into the ENR matrix, because the water in wet lignin could depress the H-bonding interaction among lignin particles, simultaneously, the ENR could interact with lignin through H-bonding and ring-opening interaction, therefore, facilitate the lignin dispersion with ENR matrix.

The visual appearances of the rubber samples are shown in Fig. 4 A. The gradual increase of transparency for the rubber films can be observed when increasing the CCDs content. Demonstrating the silica dispersion within rubber matrix is improved upon adding CCDs. Such a result might be due to the fact that some CCDs particles could be inserted among silica particles, which could reduce the re-aggregation of silica particles, Moreover, it is interesting to note that the ENR/CCDs emitted bright blue fluorescence when exposed to UV radiation. In contrast, the ENR sample exhibit weak fluorescence. Generally, the CCDs show an aggregation-induced luminescence quenching phenomenon, [24] therefore the strong fluorescence of ENR/silica/CCDs sample could demonstrate that the CCDs are uniformly dispersed in rubber matrix.

In order to quantitively study the light transmittance of the rubber composites, the rubber film scraps (thickness of 0.2 mm, shown in Fig. 4C) prepared in the rubber vulcanization process are measured using UV spectrophotometer, it is observed that the light transmittance of the ENR/silica/CCDs films are increased with the increase of CCDs (Fig. 4E), for instance, the transmittance of ENR/CCDs-8 at wavelength of 500 nm is 20.8 %, which is much higher than that of ENR (1.2 %) and ENR/4020 (0.5 %). Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 4D and F, it is observed that the incorporation of CCDs could endow the rubber films with charming fluorescence, further demonstrating the CCDs are uniformly dispersed in the rubber matrix. The intriguing fluorescence of the elastomer composites might find potential applications in the fields of UV-shielding, fluorescent tensile sensing, and chemical sensing [12,25].

SEM investigation was carried out to study the filler dispersion of ENR composites. As shown in Fig. 5 , it is observed that the filler was largely uniformly dispersed in all of the samples. This is because the epoxy group of ENR could interact with filler (silica or CCDs) through H-bonding or epoxy ring-opening reaction, promoting the filler dispersion within rubber matrix. However, in the samples of ENR and ENR/4020, we could observe that there are small amounts of silica tended to agglomerate because of strong H-bonding interactions among silica particles. Interestingly, with the incorporation of CCDs, the agglomeration of silica within the rubber matrix is greatly alleviated. For instance, there are no obvious filler agglomeration in the samples of ENR/CCDs-5 and ENR/CCDs-8. Furthermore, the interfacial boundaries between the rubber and filler are blurred, demonstrating the enhanced interfacial bonding. The SEM results agreed well with the naked eye examination of the sample's aspect (Fig. 4A) and further demonstrated that the CCDs addition could improve the filler dispersion in the composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of CCDs on the ENR/silica composites
ce:section.ce:para: The curing characteristics of the rubber composites are shown in Fig. 6 a and Table S1, it is found that the introduction of CCDs could effectively promote the vulcanization of the rubber composites. For example, the T90 for ENR was approximately 16 min, and when increasing CCDs content, the T90 was gradually decreased to approximately 4 min for ENR/CCDs-8 composite. The T10 of the rubber composite was also reduced with the increase of CCDs, further demonstrating that CCDs could accelerate the rubber vulcanization. The ΔM of the rubber composite increased with the addition of CCDs, indicating the crosslinking density of the rubber composites were increased upon CCDs addition. The fact that the CCDs could effectively promote the rubber vulcanization might be attributed to the following origins: (i) the N-related moieties of CCDs could directly attack the cyclic S8 to form linear polysulfides which are liable to be thermally activated to generate sulphur radicals, [26] leading to the rubber vulcanization. (ii) as shown in Fig. S1, the H-bonding and ring opening reaction between CCDs and ENR macromolecular chains, making CCDs actually act as efficient crosslinks in the vulcanizates, and resulting in accelerated vulcanization. (iii) furthermore, the polar groups (OH and COOH) of CCDs could interact/graft with the polar parts (NCNH) of accelerator CZ, and the CZ. These grafted additives would take part in crosslinking reaction in the vulcanization process to form crosslinks around CCDs. Other researchers reported similar results that the polar groups (OH and COOH) could interact with the functional groups (thiol groups and disulfide bonds) of accelerator CZ [27]. This interaction resulted in graphene oxide serving as crosslinking points for the rubber composite [16,28,29].

The tensile stress–strain curves of the ENR composites with various CCDs contents (0, 2.5, 5 and 8 phr) are illustrated in Fig. 6b, and the values of tensile strength and elongation at break of the ENR composites are shown in Fig. 6c. Although the tensile strength of all ENR composites largely remained unchanged (～27 MPa). It is observed that the stress at 300 % strain (i.e., 300 % modulus) of the rubber composites are increased with the CCDs loading, for instance, the sample of ENR/CCDs-8 displays an enhanced 300 % modulus of 9.7 MPa compared to that of ENR (7.3 MPa). This is because the CCDs addition could be in favor of an improved filler dispersion within ENR matrix and thus enhance the 300 % modulus. However, the elongation at break of the ENR composites decreases with the increase of CCDs loading, which is due to fact that the CCDs addition could results in the ENR rubber with higher crosslinking density. During external loading, the high crosslinking density could suppress the flexibility, mobility, slippage, and orientation of the ENR macromolecular chains. Therefore, rubber extensibility was hindered.

To further evaluate the interaction between filler and rubber matrix, the tensile stress–strain curves was analyzed by the Mooney-Rivlin equation as shown below.
σ
∗
=
σ
λ -
λ
- 2
= 2
C 1
+ 2
C 2
λ
- 2
where σ* is the reduced stress, and σ is the applied stress, λ is the extension ratio of deformed and undeformed length, and C1 and C2 are constants which are independent of λ [30]. All the curves exhibit a ''U'' shape, the upturn of the Mooney-Rivlin plot could be used to evaluate the limited extensibility of the rubber chains [31] and the interfacial adherence between filler-rubber. Generally, the upturn occurs at a lower λ (corresponding to higher λ-l) means a stronger interaction between rubber-filler [32]. As shown in Fig. 6d, the rubber composites containing CCDs exhibited upturns at lower extension ratios compared to rubber composites without CCDs, demonstrating an enhanced rubber-filler interaction upon CCDs addition. This result could probably be attributed to the following origins: (i) CCDs have large amounts of hydroxyl, amino and carboxy groups, which has a strong rubber-CCDs interaction. (ii) the CCDs could improve the overall filler dispersion in the ENR-silica-CCDs system, which effectively promoted rubber-filler interaction. Fig. 6e displays the storage modulus (G')-strain curves of the gum rubber composites, it is observed that, with the increase of strain amplitude, G' of all rubber samples decrease dramatically, this is because the filler networks would be destroyed when the strain reaches a high strain amplitude and can be explained by Payne effect [33]. The lower ΔG' value indicates superior filler dispersion, lower filler-filler interaction, and stronger filler-rubber interaction [34]. It is observed that, compared to the ENR samples without CCDs, the G'-strain curves of the ENR composites containing CCDs have longer G' plateau and lower G' values at low strain, which further demonstrate that the CCDs addition could improve the overall filler dispersion in ENR matrix. The Tan δ-strain curves of the rubber vulcanizates are shown in Fig. 6f, the Tan δ value increased with the strain because the large strain could result in stronger hysteresis loss of the rubber composite. It is well known that the Tan δ value at the strain of 7 % of vulcanizates could be an indicator for tire rolling resistance [35]. It is obvious that the CCDs addition could decrease the Tan δ value and the hysteresis loss of the ENR composites, showing great advantages in tire application in term of reducing fuel consumption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-aging properties of the rubber composites
ce:section.ce:para: The CCDs possess large amount of surface moieties (e.g., NH2, OH, and CONH- groups) with proton-donating capacity, these functional group could quench reactive radicals [30]. The radical quenching capability of CCDs was assessed by Fenton reaction (Fig. 7 a), in detail, CCDs were introduced into the salicylic acid (SA)-Fe2+–H2O2 system at concentrations ranging from 0 to 1.2 mg/ml. In this system, H2O2 undergoes decomposition into H2O and O2 catalyzed by iron ions, cycling between Fe2+ and Fe3+ [13]. SA was employed to detect Fe3+ because the Fe3+/SA complex exhibits a notable color with a UV absorption at 530 nm in the UV–vis spectra. Upon CCDs addition, the absorption peak gradually diminished (Fig. 7a and b), indicating excellent radical scavenging capability for reactive oxygen species of CCDs. Fig. 7c displays the stress–strain curves of the rubber samples after 3 days ageing. Compared Fig. 7c (after ageing) and Fig. 6b (before ageing), it is obvious that the mechanical performances of the rubber composites deteriorated seriously after the accelerated aging process, and the CCDs addition could endow the rubbers with higher retention of mechanical performance, such as tensile strength, elongation at break, and high aging coefficient (Fig. 7d and e). Also, the CCDs addition retard the increase of crosslinking density and Shore A hardness of the rubber composites (Fig. 7f and g). Fig. 7h shows the peak temperature of the rubber samples (under O2 atmosphere), it is observed that the incorporation of CCDs could effectively decrease the peak temperature in the rubber oxidation process. Although the peak temperature of rubber composites containing CCDs are lower compared to the sample of ENR/4020, the oxidation exothermic peak of the rubber composites containing CCDs are more gentle and smooth compare to ENR/4020, indicating that the oxidation reaction of rubber composites containing CCDs is not severe as that of ENR/4020. All of these have shown that the CCDs could be a promising antioxidant for elastomers.

The possible antioxidative mechanism is shown in Fig. 8 . Generally, rubber aging processes have three distinct phases: (i) Initiation, (ii) Propagation, and (iii) Termination. When introduced CCDs, the possible anti-aging process can be shown as follows:

Where R, R• and ROO• are the alkyl, alkyl radical and peroxyl radical, respectively, and ROR, ROOR and ROOA refer to the non-radical products. The plausible mechanism of excellent antioxidative capability of CCDs towards rubber are proposed as follows: CCDs are a mixture of fluorescent molecules, polymer dots and carbon dots. Firstly, the sp2–hybridized carbon core of carbon dots could stabilize free radical intermediates in rubber aging process through resonance delocalization of the electron [2]. Secondly, the abundant of functional groups such as hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on fluorescent molecules, polymer dots and carbon dots in CCDs could scavenge free radicals in rubber aging process through hydrogen atom supply. Accordingly, CCDs could effectively delay the rubber thermal-oxidative aging process.

Our results show that, the crude carbon dots without any purification can be used as multifunctional rubber additives for epoxy natural rubber/silica system by conventional milling process. The crude carbon dots could not only endow the rubber with charming fluorescence and excellent anti-aging capability, but also improve the curing rate and mechanical performances and dynamic performances of ensuing composite. We expect this work might provide new insight to develop novel multifunctional additives for diene rubbers, which is in favor of the preparation of novel and high-performance elastomer composites.

Chaoying Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Liwei Li: Investigation. Haijun Ji: Investigation. Hui Yang: Investigation. Guangzhi Jin: Investigation. Can Jiang: Investigation. Pingyun Guo: Investigation. Liqun Zhang: Investigation. Peng Yu: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Runguo Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFC2104702), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51988102, 52273003, 52103065) and the scientific research fund of Wuhan Institute of Technology (grant number K2021044) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.compositesa.2024.108177.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in the rubber antioxidants: A review
